one_liner: A 45-year-old male with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back for 12 hours.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports sudden onset of severe, constant epigastric pain that began approximately 12 hours ago after a binge drinking episode. The pain radiates straight through to his back and is worsened by eating and lying flat. He has had multiple episodes of nausea and vomiting with bilious content. He denies any fevers, hematemesis, melena, or recent trauma. He reports drinking 6-8 beers daily for the past 10 years with heavier intake over the past weekend. He has had one prior episode of similar but milder pain two years ago that resolved spontaneously.
stage2.pmh: Alcohol use disorder, hypertriglyceridemia, prior episode of epigastric pain (not formally diagnosed)
stage2.medications: None — patient does not take any regular medications
stage2.family_history: Father with alcoholic liver disease, mother with type 2 diabetes mellitus
stage2.social_history: Drinks 6-8 beers daily for the past 10 years. Smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day. Works as a construction worker. Denies illicit drug use. Lives alone.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Patient appears in significant distress, lying still and leaning forward**. HEENT: **Sclera anicteric, mucous membranes dry**. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: **Decreased breath sounds at the left base, no wheezes**. Abdomen: Marked epigastric tenderness with guarding, decreased bowel sounds, no rebound tenderness, no Grey Turner or Cullen signs. Extremities: No edema. Skin: **Warm, mildly diaphoretic**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Greater than 3x the upper limit of normal; diagnostic of the suspected condition
Elevated but less specific than lipase for this condition
Elevated suggesting dehydration; used in Ranson criteria
Mildly elevated likely due to prerenal azotemia from volume depletion
Hyperglycemia is a marker of severity in this condition
Mildly elevated; can be seen in this condition or chronic alcohol use
Mildly elevated; ALT less than 150 makes gallstone this condition less likely
Elevated but below 1000; not likely the primary cause of this episode
Mild hypocalcemia; can occur in this condition due to fat saponification
Elevated LDH is a Ranson criterion at admission

imaging_results.result: Diffuse enlargement and edema of the pancreas with peripancreatic fat stranding and small amount of peripancreatic fluid. No pancreatic necrosis identified. No gallstones or biliary dilation.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Findings consistent with acute interstitial edematous this condition without necrosis

imaging_results.result: No gallstones identified. Common bile duct measures 4 mm (normal). Liver shows mildly increased echogenicity.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: hepatic steatosis likely from alcohol use

stage5.final_clue: The patient mentions that his prior episode of similar pain two years ago also occurred after a weekend of heavy drinking and resolved after he stopped eating for a day.

debrief.classic_presentation: Acute pancreatitis classically presents with sudden onset of severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, often accompanied by nausea and vomiting. The pain is typically worsened by eating and lying supine, and improved by leaning forward. Alcohol and gallstones account for approximately 80% of cases.
debrief.pathophysiology: Alcohol causes direct toxic injury to pancreatic acinar cells and promotes premature activation of trypsinogen to trypsin within the pancreas. This triggers autodigestion of pancreatic tissue, leading to local inflammation, edema, and potentially necrosis. The inflammatory cascade can cause systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) and multi-organ dysfunction in severe cases.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is primarily supportive: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (goal-directed, typically lactated Ringer's at 5-10 mL/kg/hr initially), pain control (typically IV opioids such as hydromorphone), NPO initially with early oral feeding as tolerated (within 24 hours if able), and monitoring for complications. Alcohol cessation counseling is critical. Antibiotics are not indicated unless there is confirmed infected necrosis.
high_yield_pearls: Lipase is more sensitive and specific than amylase for acute pancreatitis and remains elevated longer
Diagnosis requires 2 of 3 criteria: characteristic abdominal pain, lipase >3x upper limit of normal, or characteristic imaging findings
Early aggressive fluid resuscitation with lactated Ringer's is the cornerstone of initial management
Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended; antibiotics should only be used for confirmed infected necrosis
sources: UpToDate: Clinical manifestations and diagnosis of acute pancreatitis — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Clinical+manifestations+and+diagnosis+of+acute+pancreatitis

after_stage2_more_likely: Acute pancreatitis (heavy alcohol use, epigastric pain radiating to back, worse with eating, leaning forward for relief)
after_stage2_less_likely: Acute cholecystitis (pain is epigastric not RUQ, no Murphy sign)
Inferior MI (no chest pain, age is young, pain clearly abdominal)
